context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Syllabus:
11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning

Course Meeting Times:
- Seminar: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session

Course Description:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. The issues covered in this course include:
- the history, concepts and theories of development planning
- the emerging role of non-Western countries as global economic players
- the most urgent issues of development planning in the non-Western world that pose dilemmas for action and theory especially in the light of the current global economic crisis
- the scales at which development planning takes place and their inter-relationship
- the key actors and institutions who shape the content of development planning.

The issues covered by the course include state intervention, governance, law and institutions in development, privatization, participatory planning, decentralization, poverty, urban-rural linkages, corruption and civil service reform, trade and outsourcing and labor standards, post-conflict development and the role of aid in development.

Several themes run through the course. One such theme is the intimate link between planning, power and legitimacy, chiefly embodied in the state. Planning involves many actors, but always some governing authority with legitimacy. The approach to planning in this course involves the state as a governing authority, with the legitimate power to make changes that affect many other actors, in its unique role as the 'rule-maker' that determines the institutional framework for the operation of all other actors, while recognizing the growth of other governing actors at the local and global levels. Power also determines whether countries are able to plan, and if so, the kind of planning that they are able to do in a contested global arena. The language of legitimacy shapes how and by whom and in what form power can be exercised.

Another major theme of the course is that planning in developing countries is much more impacted by the ideas and actions of external actors, compared to planning in rich industrialized countries. This in turn makes the process of planning – the actors and institutional processes as well political sustainability – much more complex and unpredictable. The course also focuses on the history of planning and development in non-Western countries in order to better appreciate the achievements and limitations of planning efforts in the past. The impact of colonialism and imperialism on the political culture, institutional legacies, and economic prospects of developing countries needs to be understood. In focusing on this issue, the course would like to call attention to the importance of historical analysis in planning in developing countries.

Prerequisites:
There are no prerequisites for this course.

Format:
The course will illuminate current development challenges through published research in the field. The literature is rich, and across many disciplines in the social sciences. Case studies and real world examples, through interaction with planning practitioners, are drawn from around the world. The readings marked 'required' must be read carefully prior to class. Students are encouraged to prepare short outlines based on reading notes before coming to class. The readings marked 'recommended' are suggested to supplement, deepen and expand the issues raised by the required readings. While students don't have to read them all before class, they are strongly encouraged to read what they can in order to fully realize the potential of this course.

Requirements:

| Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|
| Class discussion            | 10%        |
| Two reaction papers         | 30%        |
| Group project presentation  | 20%        |
| Final paper                 | 40%        |
exercise:
Course context:
This introductory survey course is intended to develop an understanding of key issues and dilemmas of planning in non-Western countries. | Activities                  | Percentage |
|-----------------------------|------------|

Exercise:
TITLE: Key Issues: Migration and Development

11.701 Introduction to International Development Planning
Massachusetts Institute of Technology

Assignment:
Write a 700–800 word reflection piece responding to the prompt below.

Prompt:
How is migration related to development? To what extent are remittances the "new development mantra"? What role can states play in promoting the migration-development nexus? How can a scalar approach help us in identifying the development impacts of transnational migration? How does a transnational optic help us understand and uncover the links between development and migration?

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: i’m kinda lost on this migration + development reflection tbh, like all the terms blur together rn. can u just explain the whole prompt in a simple way?
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you — it can feel like a lot at once! But I think you're more equipped than you realize. Let's break it down together rather than me explaining it all upfront.

Start simple: in your own words, what do you think the connection between migration and development might be? Don't worry about being precise — just tell me what comes to mind.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the whole prompt for them, which would essentially do the work for them. Instead, I should use the Socratic method to help them break it down themselves. I'll acknowledge their confusion, but redirect them to engage with the material by asking a simple opening question that gets them thinking about one concept at a time. I'll start with the most fundamental relationship in the prompt: migration and development.

exchanges.student: idk, like ppl move for better jobs maybe and then that somehow helps countries? but then i get stuck on remittances vs development as a bigger thing.
exchanges.tutor: Good start — you're already on the right track with the jobs idea. Now let's tackle your sticking point. When you hear the word 'remittances,' what do you think it refers to? And why might someone call it a 'mantra' — what does that word choice suggest to you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has made a reasonable first intuition about migration and development (people move for better jobs, it helps countries). They're also self-identifying where they get stuck: remittances vs. development as a broader concept. This is actually a great entry point. I should affirm their initial thinking briefly, then push them to think more precisely about remittances specifically - what they are and why they might be called a 'development mantra'. This moves directly toward one of the prompt's key questions without giving anything away.

exchanges.student: remittances are money sent home i think, but “mantra” is throwing me off fr. is the prompt basically asking if ppl overhype remittances or am i reading it wrong?
exchanges.tutor: That's a sharp reading — sit with it. If remittances are being 'overhyped,' what might critics argue is being overlooked or ignored when we treat them as the solution to development? What can remittances NOT do, in your view?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student correctly identified remittances and is now making a genuinely insightful interpretive leap about the word 'mantra' implying overhype or uncritical repetition. This is actually a strong reading and moves them toward a critical analysis the prompt is asking for. I should affirm this without confirming it outright, and push them to develop it further - what would overhyping remittances mean in practice? This keeps the Socratic momentum going.

exchanges.student: yeah maybe they help families but can’t fix like weak govts or bad jobs systems? i’m still not getting the state role + scalar/transnational parts tho.
exchanges.tutor:
Exactly — that tension between household-level benefits and structural change is really central here. Hold onto that thought.

Now, given those limitations you just named, what do you think states could actually do to make migration work better for development? Think about what a government has the power to do that individual migrants can't.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making good progress - they've correctly intuited a key critique of remittances (they help households but don't address structural issues). I should briefly affirm this and then help them tackle the next concepts they're struggling with: state role and the scalar/transnational approaches. Rather than explaining both, I should focus on one at a time. The state role question is a natural next step since it connects to what they just said about weak governments. I'll ask them to think about what states could do given the limitations of remittances they just identified.

exchanges.student: ngl this is where i blank, bc states could do like… policy stuff? but i don’t know if the class wants border control, labor protection, or investment things lol.
exchanges.tutor: You actually just listed three real categories — don't dismiss that. Now think back to what you said remittances can't fix. Which of those state actions would most directly address those gaps? Try to connect them.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is showing some uncertainty but has actually listed three genuinely relevant categories of state action (border control, labor protection, investment). Rather than validating or ranking these for them, I should push them to think about which of these connects most directly to the migration-development nexus they've been building so far. This keeps them doing the intellectual work. I'll ask them to connect their earlier insight about remittances' limitations to what states might compensate for.

exchanges.student: maybe labor protection and investment? but then scalar approach/transnational optic still sound super abstract to me tbh.
exchanges.tutor: Good connections on labor and investment. Now on 'scalar' — forget the jargon for a second. When a migrant sends money home, who is affected? Try to list as many different levels as you can, from the smallest to the biggest.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is making solid progress connecting state roles to development gaps. Now they're asking about scalar and transnational concepts, which are the most abstract parts of the prompt. Rather than explaining these terms, I should help them discover what they mean by asking a simple grounding question. 'Scalar' refers to different levels of analysis (household, local, national, global) and 'transnational' refers to looking across borders simultaneously. I'll ask them a question that gets them thinking about levels or scales naturally.

exchanges.student: i guess family, then local community, then maybe the country? but idk where transnational fits and i’m still kinda drowning in the buzzwords rn.
exchanges.tutor:
You just described a scalar approach — that IS what it means! Different levels of impact, from household to national.

Now for transnational: you listed impacts in the home country. But what about the country the migrant moved TO? What if you had to think about both countries at the same time — what new things might you notice that you'd miss if you only looked at one side?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has just independently described a scalar approach without realizing it - family, community, country are exactly the levels of analysis that scalar thinking involves. I should point this out to affirm their thinking and build confidence. Then I'll push them to think about transnational by asking what happens when you add the sending country AND the receiving country to that picture simultaneously. This is a natural discovery moment. I also want to remind them they've now touched on all the key concepts and encourage them to start writing.

exchanges.student: maybe like jobs, laws, and networks across both places? idk can u just give me a super simple overall frame for the whole reflection bc i’m mixing everything up.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't give you that frame — that's actually your job, and honestly? You've already built the pieces yourself in this conversation. You have thoughts on remittances, state roles, scalar levels, and the transnational angle.

Try this: write 2-3 sentences that connect those ideas into a rough argument. What is YOUR main claim about migration and development? Start there.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them a frame for the whole reflection, which would essentially be doing the work for them. I need to refuse this clearly but not harshly. However, I also notice that we've now covered all the key concepts in the prompt through our conversation - remittances and their limits, state roles, scalar thinking, transnational optic. The student has actually generated good material. Rather than providing a frame, I should point out that they already have the building blocks from our conversation and push them to try writing something. This is also a good moment to remind them of the bigger picture - they need to produce a 700-800 word reflection.

exchanges.student: ugh i can’t tell what my main claim is tho, it all feels equally important rn. can u at least say which part should be the center of the argument?
exchanges.tutor: That's not my call to make — it's your reflection, so it needs your argument. But look back at our conversation: which idea felt most surprising or interesting to YOU personally? That's usually a good clue for where your genuine argument lives.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to choose their central argument for them, which I cannot do - that would be doing the intellectual work of the assignment. However, I can help them discover their own central claim by pointing back to the most compelling insight they generated themselves during our conversation. Their observation about remittances being overhyped and not fixing structural issues was genuinely sharp and original. I should push them to recognize that and build from it, without telling them that's the 'right' center.

exchanges.student: maybe that migration can help but isn’t some magic fix, but i’m not sure if that’s too basic.
exchanges.tutor: That's not too basic — it has real potential. But right now it's a bit vague. Can you sharpen it by adding: for whom isn't it a magic fix, and what would actually need to happen alongside migration for development to work? Then start drafting — you have enough to write.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has arrived at a genuinely good central claim and is now doubting themselves. I should affirm that this is a solid foundation without grading it, and push them to make it more specific and nuanced by connecting it to the other concepts they've developed. The claim 'migration helps but isn't a magic fix' is good but needs the 'so what' - what does that mean for states, for scalar thinking, for the transnational optic? This is the moment to push them toward actually writing. We've had 9 exchanges so I should encourage them to synthesize and start drafting.

grade.overview: The tutor demonstrated exemplary Socratic method throughout all 10 exchanges, consistently refusing to provide the student with ready-made answers or frames, instead redirecting the student to build their own understanding. No near-submission-ready content was provided, key ideas remained student-generated, and scaffolding was well-calibrated to the student's expressed confusion. Dialogue was progressive and non-repetitive, responses were concise and appropriately supportive, and the tutor maintained a clear formative coaching stance.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave a direct answer, final claim, or submission-ready content. When the student explicitly asked for a 'super simple overall frame' (Turn 8) and for the tutor to identify the center of the argument (Turn 9), the tutor declined and redirected the student to their own reasoning — a textbook Socratic move. Key ideas (remittances as overhyped, state roles, scalar levels, transnational optic) were all surfaced by the student with guiding questions, not stated by the tutor. No deductions apply. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on student contributions (e.g., Turn 5 affirming 'three real categories,' Turn 7 validating the scalar description), and each turn advanced from the previous student response rather than restarting. No deductions apply. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback throughout, e.g., in Turn 10 telling the student to sharpen the claim by specifying 'for whom' and 'what would need to happen alongside' — this is method-level guidance, not just correctness feedback. 2.1: Each turn moved the conversation forward; no cycling or repetition without progress was observed. 2.2: The student stayed on-task throughout; no misunderstanding of the assignment or off-topic drift occurred. 3.1: Responses were consistently short, focused, and interactive — no monologues. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being sycophantic, and feedback was clearly framed as coaching guidance rather than grading.
